text: This video demonstrates robotic default lymph node dissection for rectal cancer. A sixty-eight-year-old man had bowel habit changes and tenesimus for two months. Colonoscopy showed an ulcerative mass with a half circumference in the rectum, around eight centimeters above the anal verge. MRI showed uneven wall thickening and focal extramural invasion that left anterior aspect with involvement of the mesorectal fascia. In addition, a six-millimeter regional lymph node at the mesorectum and several enlarged paraeotic lymph nodes were noted. PET findings were compatible with rectal cancer with bilateral paraeotic nodal metastasis.

The patient underwent neoadjuvant concurrent chemoradiotherapy followed by robotic-assisted TME and paraeotic D-IV lymph node dissection with ileostomy. The patient underwent total mesorectal excision and para-aortic lymph node dissection.

The surgeon begins dissection from the visceral peritoneum medially, identifying the appendix and right ureter. The ureter is mobilized and located anterior to the descending duodenum, with the gonadal vessels also mobilized. The ureter enters the pelvis anterior to the bifurcation of the common iliac artery. Lymph nodes and fatty tissue are resected around the external and internal iliac arteries. Para-aortic lymph node dissection is performed, including the area around the aorta and inferior vena cava, which runs parallel to the aorta on the right. The wall of the inferior vena cava is noted to be thinner and less elastic compared to the aorta, requiring careful dissection to avoid injury.
The surgeon dissects lymph nodes in the area of the proximal common iliac vessels, followed by dissection around the iliac bifurcation.

The surgeon continues dissection at the plane between the colon mesentery and the retroperitoneum, moving both cranially and caudally toward the lateral attachment of the colon. The tissue exhibits edematous changes and fibrosis, likely due to previous neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy, making ureter identification challenging amidst severe inflammation.
The surgeon carefully dissects to reveal the left ureter. The origin of the inferior mesenteric artery from the abdominal aorta is explored, followed by clipping and transection of the artery.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral retro-mesenteric dissection along the pre-aortic plane and genital fascia. The inferior mesenteric band is clipped and transected at the lower border of the pancreas.

The surgical resection was carried out six weeks after new adjuvant concurrent chemoradiotherapy. An additional radiation dose of four thousand five hundred centigrade was delivered to the pelvic and para-aortic area, leading to severe inflammation in the pelvic cavity. After all the surgical procedures, para-aortic lymph node dissection in addition to the standard low anterior resection was completed. Surgical pathology examination revealed residual adenocarcinoma with metastasis on one regional lymph node, as well as bilateral para-aortic lymph node metastasis.

Direct tumor regression, grade three. The operation time was five hours and thirty minutes. There was no surgical complication and a length of post-operative hospital stay was fifteen days. No local recurrence was found after six months. In summary, by using the DaVinci robotic system, high-quality surgical D-for lymph node dissection for the treatment of rectal cancer patients with abdominal periaortic lymph node metastasis can be performed precisely and safely.
